content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CorvIIIssIoN
P.O. Box 807
. 428 J Street . Sacramento. CA 958 12-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
May 18, 1999
Stephen N. Cool
Law Office of Stephen N. Cool
1577 El Camino Real
Arroyo Grande, California 93420
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-084
Dear Mr. Cool:
This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalfof the Avila Beach
Community Foundation, regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
Please keep in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that
has afready taken place.
2 In addition, this letter is solely based on the facts presented to us in
your letter, and in our telephone conversation ofApril 27, 1999. The Commission does not act
as a finder of fact when issuing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Our advice is
applicable only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material
facts have been provided.
QUESTION
Are the members ofthe board of trustees ofthe Avila Beach Community Foundation
members of a public agency who must file statements of economic interests?
CONCLUSION
The members ofthe board of trustees of the Avila Beach Community Foundation are
members of a public agency who must file statements of economic interests, as prescribed by the
agency’s conflict of interest code.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Pursuant to regulation 18329, the Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct.
(Regulation 1 8329(b)(8)(A).)


File No. A-99-084
Page 2
FACTS
The Avila Beach Community Foundation (“Foundation”) is a nonprofit public benefit
corporation. The Foundation was created in 1998 by the Avila Beach Community Services
District (“District”), a government agency.
The creation ofthe Foundation was prompted by the settlement of a contamination
lawsuit against UNOCAL. The District was not a party to the suit, but allegations made against
UNOCAL in the suit involved Avila Beach. The parties to the suit agreed, as part of the
settlement, that UNOCAL would pay a substantial amount ofmoney to a public benefit
corporation, that would utilize the funds for community improvements in Avila Beach. Avila
Beach did not have a public benefit corporation to receive the funds, however, so the board of
directors of the District stepped in. The board of directors utilized its staffto create the
Foundation, because there was no other entity in the town that was in a position to do so. The
Foundation was created as a public benefit corporation that would receive the settlement funds,
and use the funds for general community purposes. All of the expenses incurred by the District,
in the creation of the Foundation, were later reimbursed to the District from the Foundation’s
funds.
Under the Foundation’s By-Laws, the Foundation is governed by a ten-member board of
trustees. Five of the trustees are the five directors of the District. The other five trustees are
residents or landowners in Avila Beach, who are each appointed by a District director. You have
advised the board of trustees that their meetings must comply with the requirements of the
Brown Act, inasmuch as the Board membership includes all of the District directors, and it is
possible that District business could be discussed at meetings of the trustees.
The Foundation has now received from UNOCAL, all ofthe settlement funds that it will
receive. It is expected, however, that the Foundation will receive additional funds through gifts,
bequests, and community fund raising activities. The Foundation has not received, and is not
expected to receive, any funding from any public agencies.
The purpose for which the Foundation may expend its funds is set forth in Article II of
the Foundation’s Articles ofIncorporation. This Article declares that the money and property
received by the Foundation shall be used “to fund repair and/or improvement projects for the
general public benefit of the community ofAvila Beach.” Under this broad declaration of
purpose, some of the Foundation’s funds could be allocated for physical improvements to
facilities or equipment operated by the District, such as improvement of the District’s water and
sewer systems, or improvement ofthe District’s fire protection equipment.
The five Foundation trustees who are also directors ofthe District complete annual
statements of economic interests, due to their positions as District directors. You want to know
whether the other five trustees, who are not District directors, are also required to complete
statements of economic interests, simply because they are Foundation trustees.


File No. A-99-084
Page 3
ANALYSIS
Section 87300 of the Act requires every state and local agency to adopt a conflict of
interest code applicable to its “designated employees.” For the purposes of Section 87300,
“agency” is understood to mean any state agency or local government agency. (Maas Advice
Letter, No. A-98-261.) “Local government agency” is defined in Section 82041 as:
“... a county, city or district of any kind including school district,
or any other local or regional political subdivision, or any
department, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other
agency ofthe foregoing.”
Whether a particular entity is a private entity, or a local government agency, is essentially
a factual determination, that must be made on a case-by-case basis. (In re Vonk (1981) 6 FPPC
Ops. 1.) As a way ofmaking that determination a little easier, the Commission has adopted a
four-part factual test for deciding whether a particular entity is a local government agency. (In re
Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62.) Under this “Siegel test,” the following four criteria must be
considered:
(1) Whether the impetus for formation ofthe entity originated
with a government agency;
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary
source of funds is, a government agency;
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the entity
was formed is to provide services or undertake obligations which
public agencies are legally authorized to perform and which, in
fact, they traditionally have performed; and
(4) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other
statutory provisions.
While the Siegel test is not intended to be a definitive litmus test for determining whether an
entity is a private entity or a local government agency (Vose Advice Letter, No. 1-97-578),
through application ofits four test criteria, you can draw certain conclusions about the entity in
question, that go a long way toward completing the analysis. We will therefore apply the Siegel
test to what you have told us about the Foundation, and from there determine whether the
Foundation should be considered a local government agency.
Impetus For Formation
It was your contention in your letter requesting advice that the idea of a public benefit
corporation, like the Foundation, being formed as a receiving agency for the UNOCAL
settlement, originated with the parties to the UNOCAL contamination lawsuit. While this may
be true, it is also true that it was the District’s board of directors who decided to form the


File No. A-99-084
Page 4
Foundation to serve as that receiving agency. It was also the District’s board of directors who
chose to utilize District staffto form the Foundation. You even implied in your letter that ifthe
District had not formed the Foundation, then a public benefit corporation probably would not
have been formed, because “no other entity in town was in a position to do so.” As such, we
must find that the District, a government agency, was the impetus for the formation of the
Foundation. The first criterion ofthe Siegel test is therefore satisfied.
Funded By a Government Agency
The only financial support that we are aware ofthe Foundation receiving from a
government agency is the support that it received in the form ofDistrict resources being
expended to initially form the Foundation. We further understand that even this limited support
from the District could be characterized as a short-term loan, as the Foundation reimbursed the
District for this expenditure ofresources, out ofthe settlement funds that the Foundation received
from UNOCAL.
You did not discuss in your letter whether the District supplies anything ofvalue to the
Foundation, such as facilities for meetings, technical assistance, or the like. You also did not
discuss whether the District compensates its directors for the work that they perform as
Foundation trustees. Support ofthis kind could constitute governmental funding ofthe
Foundation (Moser Advice Letter, No. 1-97-595), although, in this case, it appears that such
funding, if it occurs, would only be a minimal supplement to the Foundation’s primary funding.
It therefore does not appear that the Foundation is being primarily, or even substantially funded
by a government agency. The second criterion ofthe Siegel test is therefore not satisfied.
Services Which Public Agencies Traditionally Peiform
In your letter, you stated that the principal purposes ofthe Foundation do not include the
provision of services, or the undertaking of obligations, that the District is authorized to perform,
and traditionally has performed. At the same time, however, you acknowledged that some of the
Foundation’s funds could be allocated for physical improvements to facilities or equipment
operated by the District, such as improvement ofthe District’s water and sewer systems, or
improvement of the District’s fire protection equipment. Furthermore, the Foundation’s Articles
of Incorporation declare that a principal purpose of the Foundation is “to fund repair and/or
improvement projects for the general public benefit ofthe community ofAvila Beach.”
Notwithstanding your contention regarding what services or undertakings the District is
authorized to perform, or has traditionally performed, it is undoubtedly true that the funding of
repair and/or improvement projects for the benefit ofthe general public is one ofthe primary
services that public agencies on the local, state, and federal level are legally authorized to
perform. It is also a service that they traditionally perform. This is most obviously true where
the facilities that are to be repaired or improved are government owned facilities, like water and


File No. A-99-084
Page 5
sewer systems, and fire protection equipment. Accordingly, the Foundation clearly satisfies this
third criterion of the Siegel test.
Treated as a Public Entity By Other Statutes
Although you contended in your letter that the Foundation is not treated as a public entity
by other statutory provisions, the Foundation, as a nonprofit public benefit corporation, enjoys
certain tax benefits similar to other public agencies. You also noted that the Foundation’s board
of trustees maybe subject to the provisions ofthe Brown Act, which applies to the meetings of
public entities. The Foundation is therefore comparable to the NAVCO Pacific Development
Corp., which in the Lober Advice Letter, No. A-90-135, we found was treated as a public entity
by other statutes. As such, the Foundation also satisfies the fourth criterion of the Siegel test.
Evaluation
After applying the Siegel test to the facts presented in your letter, we find that three of the
four criteria for an entity to be considered a local government agency are satisfied. It is not
necessary that all four ofthe criteria be satisfied for an entity to be considered a local government
agency. (O’Shea Advice Letter, No. A-91-570.) It is only necessary that the entity satisfies
enough ofthe four criteria for its overall character to correspond to that of a local government
agency.
In this instance, we conclude that the Foundation’s overall character corresponds to that
of a local government agency. The Foundation’s trustees are therefore subject to the Act’s
conflict of interest provisions, including the requirement that they file statements of economic
interest, as required in the Foundation’s conflict of interest code.
3
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
— -
By:
Steven Benito Russo
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:SBR:tls
If the Foundation needs any help with its conflict of interest code, it is encouraged to contact the
Technical Assistance Division of this agency.

members of a public agency who must file statements of economic interests?

members of a public agency who must file statements of economic interests, as prescribed by the
agency’s conflict of interest code.2 Pursuant to regulation 18329, the Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct.
(Regulation 1 8329(b)(8)(A).)

members of a public agency who must file statements of economic interests?

CONCLUSION: The members ofthe board of trustees of the Avila Beach Community Foundation are
members of a public agency who must file statements of economic interests, as prescribed by the
agency’s conflict of interest code.2 Pursuant to regulation 18329, the Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct.
(Regulation 1 8329(b)(8)(A).)
